content.text: WAI-ARIA (Web Accessibility Initiative — Accessible Rich Internet Applications) is a W3C specification that adds semantic meaning to HTML beyond what native elements provide. It communicates role, state, and property information to assistive technologies (screen readers, braille displays, switch controls) that native HTML alone cannot express — particularly for custom interactive widgets like tabs, accordions, carousels, and data grids.

attrs.alt: ARIA roles, properties and states reference — landmark/widget/document roles, static aria-* properties, and dynamic aria-* states
attrs.title: ARIA Roles, Properties and States

content.text: The first rule of ARIA use is: if there is a native HTML element or attribute that expresses the semantics you need, use it instead of ARIA. Native elements carry implicit roles and handle keyboard interaction by default; ARIA only overrides what the browser communicates to assistive technology — it does not change behavior.
Button handles Enter/Space, has implicit role button
Checkbox manages checked state, keyboard, and form submission
h2 has implicit role heading level 2, styles included
Semantic list with screen reader list navigation
ARIA is a last resort, not a first choice. Every ARIA attribute you add is markup the browser would have produced for free from the right native element.
The role attribute overrides the implicit role of an element in the accessibility tree. There are four categories.
Landmark roles create named regions that screen reader users can jump between with keyboard shortcuts. Native HTML5 sectioning elements provide these automatically.

Widget roles — building custom interactive controls
Widget roles describe interactive UI patterns. When you apply a widget role, you take on the responsibility of implementing the keyboard behavior defined in the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG).

— the tab widget pattern. Keyboard: arrow keys move between tabs, Enter/Space selects.
— modal/non-modal dialog. Must trap focus inside and return focus on close.
— application menu. Arrow keys navigate; not appropriate for navigation menus (use <nav>).
— short important message that doesn't require interaction. Announced immediately by screen readers.
— non-urgent update (like a toast notification). Announced politely when the screen reader is idle.
role="none" (synonym: "presentation") removes the element and all its descendants from the accessibility tree. Use it when a purely decorative element would add noise for screen reader users.
LogoNav
ARIA properties (static aria-* attributes) describe characteristics of an element that do not change based on user interaction. The most important are the labelling attributes.

Billing address

Must be 8+ characters with a number. Password is too short.
takes a string; it completely replaces the accessible name. Use when there is no visible text to reference.
takes one or more space-separated ids; the referenced text becomes the accessible name. Prefer this over aria-label because the label is also visible.
takes ids; the referenced text is announced as supplementary description after the label. Points to hint text and error messages.
Priority: aria-labelledby > aria-label > <label for> association > title attribute > element content.

ARIA states change as the user interacts with the page. They must be updated programmatically whenever the UI changes.
Angular is a TypeScript-based web framework…

aria-hidden="true" removes an element and its descendants from the accessibility tree without visually hiding it. Use it to hide decorative content, duplicate content, or off-screen content that is not relevant to the current interaction.

★★★☆☆ 3 out of 5 stars
…

Never add aria-hidden="true" to a focusable element — keyboard users can still tab into it, but screen readers will not announce it, creating a confusing experience. Remove from the tab order with tabindex="-1" as well.
Live regions announce content changes to screen readers without requiring the user to navigate there. Three politeness levels control when the announcement interrupts the current speech.
Content that changes but is not important to announce
Announced when the reader is idle
Status messages, search result counts, auto-save confirmations
Showing {{ filteredCount }} of {{ totalCount }} results
causes the entire region to be re-read when any part changes. Without it, only the changed portion is announced.
controls which changes trigger announcements: additions, removals, or text changes. Defaults to "additions text".
Role shortcuts: role="status" implies aria-live="polite" + aria-atomic="true". role="alert" implies aria-live="assertive" + aria-atomic="true".
Inject content into a live region after it is already in the DOM — adding the element and its content simultaneously may not be announced reliably.
aria-current marks the element that represents the current item within a set — particularly useful for navigation links and breadcrumbs.

ARIA in Angular: CDK Accessibility Module
Angular's Component Dev Kit (CDK) provides utilities that complement ARIA in Angular apps. Import from @angular/cdk/a11y.

Confirm deletion

This action cannot be undone.
prevents Tab from leaving the dialog while it is open. This is required WCAG 2.1 behavior for modal dialogs.
automatically focuses the first focusable element when the dialog opens.
When the dialog closes, manually return focus to the element that triggered it:
role="button" on a <div> tells screen readers it is a button, but the element still will not respond to Enter/Space without manual keydown listeners. Use <button>.
<button role="button"> is redundant. <input type="checkbox" aria-checked="false"> conflicts with the native checked state.
Keyboard users tab into the element but hear nothing. Add tabindex="-1" too, or remove the element from the DOM.
aria-expanded or aria-selected not updated when the UI changes. Angular's property binding ([attr.aria-expanded]="open") keeps them in sync automatically.
Over-labelling creates verbosity. Label elements that lack a clear accessible name; do not add aria-label to elements that already have descriptive visible text.
assertive interrupts the user's current reading. Reserve it for urgent errors. Use "polite" for status updates.
Without aria-modal="true", some screen readers allow virtual cursor to exit the dialog boundaries. Combined with focus trapping, aria-modal keeps users inside.
ARIA adds role, property, and state semantics to elements that native HTML cannot express — use native elements first (Rule 1 of ARIA).
Roles describe what an element is: landmark roles (banner, navigation, main), widget roles (dialog, tab, menu), and document roles (heading, list).
ARIA properties (static) communicate relationships: aria-label, aria-labelledby, aria-describedby, aria-controls.
ARIA states (dynamic) track interaction: aria-expanded, aria-selected, aria-hidden, aria-current, aria-invalid — update them whenever the UI changes.
Live regions (aria-live="polite"/"assertive") announce content changes without requiring navigation — use role="status" for updates and role="alert" for errors.
Angular's CDK provides LiveAnnouncer for programmatic announcements and cdkTrapFocus for accessible dialog focus management.
Test with a real screen reader (NVDA + Chrome on Windows, VoiceOver + Safari on macOS/iOS) — automated ARIA validation catches errors but cannot verify the experience.
